dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 2 for a seizure call at [CITY]-48M involved.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT. What happened to the patient today?
He suddenly collapsed and had a seizure. It lasted about 45 seconds.
He's awake but confused and unable to respond to questions clearly.
Respiratory rate was 20, later dropped to 18 and stayed stable during transport prep.
Initial SpO2 was unmeasurable, improved to 93% with O2 and later to 98%.
BP was undetectable initially, improved to 106/54 and later 100/64.
Pulse was unobtainable at first, then rose to 109 post-midazolam and trended downward to 99.
EKG was unreadable at first, showed sinus tach, and resolved to sinus rhythm.
Circulation is intact with no signs of trauma observed initially.
Skin appears normal with no rashes, bruising, or injuries.
No bite marks, petechiae, or other skin irregularities found.
Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light.
Does he have any known conditions or medications?
He has epilepsy and takes levetiracetam regularly. Also, diabetes with insulin dependence.
Is he currently on any other medications, especially for his diabetes or seizures?
He's been taking his anti-seizure meds, but not sure if dosing was on time.
Any alcohol or other substances prior to the seizure?
We found some empty ETOH cans, so he might have consumed alcohol.
When was the last time he ate or consumed anything prior to this event?
Mostly alcohol-no solid food seen. He might have skipped meals.
Applying 4-lead ECG monitoring to check cardiac rhythm before administration.
EKG confirmed sinus tach and then returned to normal sinus rhythm.
Pulse remained at 109 just before drug administration, declined slightly afterward.
BP improved and stabilized during ongoing assessments.
Starting IV access in left forearm with 18G for medication and fluids.
EKG remains stable with no new findings after IV placement.
Chest expansion is symmetrical and unlabored with no dyspnea.
Adding capnography for continuous respiration monitoring during interventions.
Following Medical Protocol 3-12 for seizures and General Protocol 3-4 for behavioral management.
Ready the stretcher-the patient is combative, so we need secure transport.
Place in semi-Fowler's to aid breathing and comfort during transfer to the ambulance.
Patient has no recorded facility preference. Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME].
No preference-he will go to [COMPANY_NAME] since it's the closest with security.
SpO2 maintained at 98% with 3LNC on board, continuously tracked.
BP is at 101/52 and pulse is at 105-noted downward trend, no acute concerns.
Vitals appear stable with ongoing ECG and O2 monitoring.
Ongoing monitoring with EKG and O2 is part of baseline care.
Administered 5mg Midazolam IM in left deltoid for aggressive behavior.
EKG now consistent with sinus rhythm, no new findings before transport.
5mg of Midazolam given-no adverse event observed before securing.
Patient restrained with soft cravats due to risk of harm to self or crew.
All equipment secured-ready for transfer with IV, O2, and EKG in place.
SpO2 at 95% and vitals stable, no signs of repeated seizure noted.
Latest vitals: HR 99, BP 100/64 just before handover.
Oxygen saturation remains stable with no change in clinical status.
He remains in semi-Fowler's position with no acute decline noted before exit.
GCS 6 (E1V1M4) following Midazolam as recorded during final stages of care.
Glucose measured at 86 mg/dL, within expected range given possible ETOH.
Patient did not show signs of pain or discomfort during transport or assessment.
Briefing ED with details including ETOH use, seizures, Midazolam, IV, and GCS.
Arrived at [COMPANY_NAME]-handover to ED staff as per protocol for seizure patient.
Handoff complete-patient transferred to ED staff after initial stabilization.
He's on Albuterol and other inhalers too-should have been in the notes from the start.
No missed medications noted-levetiracetam and metformin are being used.
EMS followed Protocol 3-12 and 3-4 for seizure and behavioral management.
